misconceptionSummary: Students compare digits in the wrong order or focus on digit count rather than place value.

steps.narration: The student is comparing individual digits, not the numbers as a whole. Looking at the leading nine versus the leading one completely ignores what column each digit is in.

visuals.ruleText: More digits = larger number (when there are no leading zeros)

steps.narration: Before comparing any individual digits, count how many digits each number has. More digits always wins — you don't need to look any further.

lines.text: Same digit count → compare left to right

steps.narration: Only when the digit counts match do you compare column by column, starting from the left. Stop as soon as you find the first column that differs.

visuals.label: 4 digits beats 3 digits — no further comparison needed

steps.narration: One thousand and one beats nine hundred and ninety-nine purely because it has an extra digit. The nine in the hundreds column never even comes into it.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: comparing individual digits instead of digit counts first. Always count the digits before comparing column by column.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: If one number has more digits, you already know it's bigger. Don't let a large-looking digit in a small number fool you.
